food_subgroup: Whole grains — cooked millet; pearl millet (Pennisetum glaucum), boiled/cooked; 1 bowl = 180g

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for cooked pearl millet as a food product; SNOMED CT coverage for individual cooked grains is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of cooked pearl millet weighs approximately 180g and provides 214 kcal, 6.3g protein, 42.7g carbohydrate, and 79.2mg magnesium (approximately 19–20% of adult RDA).

nutrient_source.title: Millet, Pearl — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID: 169702)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values correspond to cooked pearl millet per USDA SR Legacy. All per-100g values sourced directly from this entry. Serving size of 180g is a standard bowl portion consistent with Singapore dietary reference.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — pearl millet, cooked
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. (2008) Diabetes Care
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI ~67 (medium) for cooked pearl millet sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database and corroborated by Atkinson FS et al. (2008) International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values. Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283. GL calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (67 × 42.66g) / 100 ≈ 28.6, rounded to 28.5 per input record. Millet starch gelatinises rapidly during cooking, explaining the medium-high GI despite being a whole grain.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (2mg/100g; ~4mg per 180g bowl) combined with high magnesium (44mg/100g; ~79mg per bowl) and moderate potassium (62mg/100g; ~112mg per bowl)
hypertension.guidance: Pearl millet is highly suitable for hypertension management: its negligible sodium content (~4mg per bowl) poses no BP risk, while its exceptionally high magnesium content (~79mg per bowl, ~19% RDA) has documented modest blood-pressure-lowering effects, and its potassium content provides additional vasodilatory benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: GI ~67 (medium) — higher than brown rice (GI ~55) and red rice (GI ~57); GL ~28.5 per 180g bowl
type2_diabetes.guidance: Despite being a whole grain, cooked pearl millet has a GI of ~67 due to rapid starch gelatinisation during cooking, making it a less optimal rice substitute for T2DM glycaemic management compared to brown rice (GI ~55) or red rice (GI ~57); portion control is recommended and it should not be assumed to be glycaemically superior to other whole grains.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.2g/100g), zero cholesterol, zero trans fat, high magnesium, and gluten-free whole grain status
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Pearl millet is cardioprotective: its very low saturated fat (0.2g/100g), zero cholesterol, zero trans fat, and high magnesium content (44mg/100g) support cardiovascular health, and its whole-grain fibre (1.3g/100g) contributes to LDL reduction when consumed as part of a balanced diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High magnesium (anti-inflammatory mineral), gluten-free, and dietary folate relevant for methotrexate-treated patients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Pearl millet is suitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients: its high magnesium content (~79mg per bowl) has anti-inflammatory relevance, it is naturally gluten-free (suitable for RA patients with concurrent coeliac disease), and its folate content (~34mcg per bowl) supports patients on methotrexate therapy where dietary folate supplementation is standard practice.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pearl millet (Pennisetum glaucum), cooked, is a small round whole grain with a mild nutty flavour and slightly chewy texture when boiled. A standard serving is one bowl (180g cooked), providing approximately 214 kcal, 6.3g protein, 1.8g fat, and 42.7g carbohydrate. It is notable for its exceptionally high magnesium content — approximately 79mg per 180g bowl (~19% adult RDA) — among the highest of any grain. Despite being a whole grain, cooked pearl millet has a glycemic index of approximately 67 (medium), which is counter-intuitively higher than brown rice (GI ~55) and red rice (GI ~57). This is because millet starch gelatinises rapidly during cooking, resulting in faster glucose absorption. For Type 2 Diabetes management, pearl millet is NOT the preferred rice substitute; brown, red, or multigrain rice are better choices for GI reduction. However, for other nutritional goals — magnesium intake, gluten-free diet, folate support, or cardiovascular health — pearl millet is an excellent choice. It is naturally gluten-free, vegan, and halal. In Singapore, pearl millet is primarily consumed within South Indian communities, used in dishes such as upma, kanji (porridge), and pongal. It is available at Indian grocery stores including Mustafa Centre and shops in Little India, and increasingly at health food stores as a gluten-free grain alternative. Known as 'kambu' in Tamil and 'bajra' in Hindi, it is a staple grain across Africa and South Asia.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (exact match for cooked pearl millet). GI value of 67 sourced from University of Sydney GI Database and corroborated by peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). Ontology codes (FoodOn, FoodEx2) are defensible direct matches. All mandatory multilingual names present. All hard gates pass.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for cooked pearl millet is not separately reported in the USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for this food item (FDC ID: 169702); monounsaturated fat data unavailable from this approved source. polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for cooked pearl millet is not separately reported in the USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for this food item (FDC ID: 169702); polyunsaturated fat data unavailable from this approved source. water_g set to -1: moisture content for cooked pearl millet is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for this food item (FDC ID: 169702); water content data unavailable from this approved source. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for cooked pearl millet. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as pearl millet is a plant-based grain with no animal-derived components. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, and vitamin_b12_mcg are 0.0 — confirmed negligible or zero per USDA SR Legacy for cooked pearl millet. Note on GI: the GI of ~67 for cooked pearl millet is higher than brown rice (GI ~55) and red rice (GI ~57), making it a less optimal choice for T2DM glycaemic management despite its whole-grain status. The description in the input record incorrectly labels the food as 'uncooked grain millet' — this record pertains to cooked pearl millet (180g bowl). The alias 'finger millet' is retained from the input but noted as technically a different species (Eleusine coracana); it is included for retrieval purposes only as users may conflate the two. The input magnesium value of 44mg/100g is used as sourced from USDA SR Legacy; the input health notes citing 79mg/100g appear to be an error in the original record (79mg/100g would be for raw/uncooked millet, not cooked).
